era_context: Continuation of the late Old Kingdom in Egypt (Dynasty VI), the Akkadian Empire under Naram-Sin in Mesopotamia, and the mature Harappan civilization in the Indus Valley. This is a period of relative stability in established empires, though underlying pressures (decentralization in Egypt, internal rebellions in Akkad) are building toward major transitions in the following centuries.

events.title: Pepi II of Egypt — continued reign and trade expeditions
events.description: Pepi II continues to sponsor trade expeditions to Nubia (for gold, incense, and ivory) and the Levant (for cedar and other goods). Autobiographical inscriptions from provincial officials (nomarchs) record these expeditions and the growing wealth and autonomy of provincial elites. The central government's control is gradually weakening as local governors accumulate power and resources. This period marks the transition from centralized Old Kingdom authority to the more decentralized system that will characterize the First Intermediate Period.
key_figures: Harkhuf, nomarch of Aswan (recorded in tomb inscriptions)

sources.name: Autobiographical inscriptions from tombs of Harkhuf, Sabni, and other nomarchs

events.certainty_note: The existence of trade expeditions under Pepi II is well-attested in contemporary inscriptions. However, the specific year -2278 is assigned by convention; the exact regnal year is uncertain.
cross_references: Part of broader Old Kingdom decline

events.title: Naram-Sin of Akkad — military campaigns and consolidation
events.description: Naram-Sin continues military campaigns to suppress rebellions within the Akkadian Empire and expand control over neighboring regions. Inscriptions record victories over the Lullubi (in the Zagros Mountains) and other groups. The empire reaches its greatest territorial extent during this period. However, evidence of internal instability and pressure from external enemies (particularly the Gutians from the northeast) is mounting.

sources.name: Akkadian royal inscriptions and victory stelae

events.certainty_note: Naram-Sin's military campaigns are well-documented in contemporary inscriptions. However, the absolute dating is uncertain by ±10–20 years depending on the chronological system used.

events.title: Harappan civilization — continued urban development and trade
events.description: The Harappan civilization continues its mature phase with active trade networks extending to Mesopotamia, the Persian Gulf, and Central Asia. Archaeological evidence shows standardized pottery, weights, and measures, suggesting either a unified state or a highly coordinated network of city-states. The undeciphered Indus script appears on seals and pottery, indicating a complex administrative system. The civilization's economy is based on agriculture (wheat, barley), animal husbandry, and craft production.

sources.name: Archaeological excavations and material culture analysis
Mesopotamian cuneiform references to trade with Meluhha

events.certainty_note: The existence and characteristics of the Harappan civilization are confirmed by archaeology. The year -2278 is assigned to the mature phase by convention.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. However, the broader historiographic issues noted for -2279 remain: the length of Pepi II's reign is disputed, Akkadian chronology is uncertain, and the Indus script remains undeciphered, limiting knowledge of Harappan political organization.
historiographic_note: The historical record for -2278 is similar to -2279: dominated by Egyptian administrative inscriptions and Akkadian royal inscriptions, with the Indus Valley known only through archaeology. The dating of all events depends on competing chronological systems. No contemporary written records exist for most of the world outside Mesopotamia and Egypt.
